title: FINRA Case 25-02261 — Ricardo Eboli-Kodesh v. UBS Financial Services Inc
text:
Expungement Award
FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
In the Matter of the Arbitration Between:
Claimant Case Number: 25-02261
Ricardo Eboli-Kodesh
vs.
Respondent Hearing Site: San Juan, Puerto Rico
UBS Financial Services Inc.
Awards are rendered by independent arbitrators who are appointed pursuant to Rule 13806 of
the Code of Arbitration Procedure (“Code”) to issue final, binding decisions. FINRA makes
available an arbitration forum—pursuant to rules approved by the SEC—but has no part in
deciding the award.
Nature of the Dispute: Associated Person vs. Member
The expungement hearing was conducted by videoconference.
REPRESENTATION OF PARTIES
For Claimant Ricardo Eboli-Kodesh (“Claimant”): Leany P. Prieto-Rodríguez, Esq. and Roberto
C. Quiñones-Rivera, Esq., McConnell Valdés LLC, San Juan, Puerto Rico.
For Respondent UBS Financial Services Inc. (“Respondent”): Rey F. Medina-Vélez, Esq.,
Sanchez/LRV LLC, San Juan, Puerto Rico.
CASE INFORMATION
Statement of Claim filed on or about: October 16, 2025.
Claimant signed the Submission Agreement: October 16, 2025.
Statement of Answer filed on or about: November 20, 2025.
Respondent signed the Submission Agreement: December 1, 2025.
CASE SUMMARY
In the Statement of Claim, Claimant asserted a claim seeking expungement of customer dispute
information from registration records maintained by the Central Registration Depository (“CRD”).
In the Statement of Answer, Respondent supported Claimant’s expungement request.

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02261
Award Page 2 of 8
RELIEF REQUESTED
In the Statement of Claim, Claimant (CRD Number 4313868) requested expungement of all
references to Occurrence Number 2270699 from the CRD registration records.
In the Statement of Answer, Respondent confirmed that, except for the filing fee, it shall be
covering any and all assessment of fees related to the request for expungement.
At the expungement hearing, Respondent made an oral unopposed request that all costs of the
expungement hearing be paid by Respondent.
OTHER ISSUES CONSIDERED AND DECIDED
The Arbitrators acknowledge that they have each read the pleadings and other materials filed by
the parties.
On February 26, 2026, Claimant advised that the customers in Occurrence Number 2270699
(“Customers”) and their representative were served with a copy of the Statement of Claim and
Statement of Answer, although not within 10 days of their filings, and provided proof of service.
Claimant also filed copies of all communications sent to the Customers and their representative
and provided proof of service.
The Panel conducted a recorded hearing by videoconference on March 12, 2026, so the parties
could present oral argument and evidence on Claimant’s request for expungement. Claimant
appeared by videoconference at the expungement hearing. Respondent appeared at the
expungement hearing and, as stated in the Statement of Answer, supported the request for
expungement.
Neither the Customers nor their representative appeared at the expungement hearing.
No authorized representative of a state securities regulator appeared at the expungement hearing.
The Panel noted Claimant’s representation that a prior arbitration panel or court has not
previously ruled on expungement of the same occurrence in the CRD.
The Panel reviewed the settlement documentation related to Occurrence Number 2270699,
considered the amount of payment made to any party to the settlement, and considered other
relevant terms and conditions of the settlement. The Panel noted that the settlement was not
conditioned on any party to the settlement not opposing the expungement request and that
Claimant did not contribute to the settlement amount.
The Panel relied upon the following documentary or other evidence: Claimant’s sworn testimony
regarding his role on the Almonte Team, his limited involvement with the Customers’ investment
decisions, and the suitability of his recommendations; the Customers’ underlying Statement of
Claim, including all allegations of unsuitability, misrepresentation, and over-concentration;
Respondent’s Statement of Answer expressly supporting expungement and confirming that the
allegations against Claimant were clearly erroneous and false; account statements, portfolio
summaries, and the Profit & Loss Report showing the Customers’ investment objectives, risk

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02261
Award Page 3 of 8
tolerance, non-discretionary account status, portfolio composition, and more than $538,000.00
in dividends and interest and over $320,000.00 in net profit; evidence regarding the 2013 Puerto
Rico bond market collapse, demonstrating that losses were caused by external market
conditions, not by Claimant’s conduct; Claimant’s current BrokerCheck® Report, confirming the
single occurrence at issue and Claimant’s disciplinary history; testimony and documents
establishing the Customers’ financial industry experience, contradicting the allegation that they
were unsophisticated investors; and evidence showing the Customers continued to work with
Claimant after the events at issue, undermining allegations of fraud or misconduct.
AWARD
After considering the pleadings, the testimony and evidence presented at the expungement
hearing, and any post-hearing submissions, the Panel has decided in full and final resolution of
the issues submitted for determination as follows:
1. The Panel awards the expungement of all references to Occurrence Number 2270699 from
registration records maintained by the CRD for Claimant Ricardo Eboli-Kodesh (CRD
Number 4313868) with the understanding that, pursuant to Rule 2080, Claimant Ricardo
Eboli-Kodesh must obtain confirmation from a court of competent jurisdiction before the CRD
will execute the expungement directive.
Unless specifically waived in writing by FINRA, parties seeking judicial confirmation of an
arbitration award containing customer dispute expungement relief must name FINRA as an
additional party and serve FINRA with all appropriate documents.
Pursuant to Rule 13805 of the Code, the Panel has made the following affirmative findings of
fact:
The claim, allegation, or information is factually impossible or clearly erroneous; and
The claim, allegation, or information is false.
The Panel has made the above findings based on the following reasons:
The Panel unanimously finds that the allegations in the Customers’ complaint are clearly
erroneous and false as to Claimant, and therefore expungement is warranted under
FINRA Rule 13805(c)(9)(A)(i)(a) and (c). The Panel’s findings are based on the
testimony, documentary evidence, and Respondent’s express concessions.
1. Customers’ allegations
The Customers alleged that:
• They were “unsophisticated investors” who relied entirely on
Respondent and Claimant.
• Respondent and Claimant “fraudulently concealed” risks of Puerto Rico
closed-end funds (CEBFs).

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02261
Award Page 4 of 8
• Claimant recommended unsuitable over-concentration in Puerto Rico
securities.
• Claimant repeatedly advised them to “hold” declining securities and
misrepresented their condition.
• Claimant solicited additional Puerto Rico bond purchases in 2019.
• These alleged actions caused more than $200,000.00 in losses.
The Panel finds that these allegations are not supported by the evidence.
2. Customers were not unsophisticated investors
The evidence showed that both Customers had significant professional
experience in the Puerto Rico financial industry, contradicting the Customers’
underlying Statement of Claim assertion that they were unsophisticated or
unable to understand investment risks. Claimant credibly testified that he
considered their background, objectives, and risk tolerance when making
recommendations. Respondent confirmed this in its Statement of Answer.
3. Claimant did not recommend over-concentration or high-risk products
The Panel finds that:
• Claimant recommended diversified, high-quality Puerto Rico AAA funds
with minimal Puerto Rico credit exposure (2.6%).
• The Customers chose to invest heavily in Puerto Rico products because
of the tax-advantaged income, not because of any misrepresentation.
• The inherited COFINA bonds were held at the Customers’ request.
• As of June 2013, only 20.5% of the Customers’ overall portfolio was
exposed to Puerto Rico credit risk.
• Respondent expressly confirmed that Claimant did not recommend
concentration and that the products were suitable for the Customers’
objectives.
4. The accounts were non-discretionary
The Customers-not Claimant-made all final investment decisions.
The Panel finds:
• Claimant provided appropriate disclosures.
• The Customers knowingly accepted the risks.
• There is no evidence that Claimant exercised discretion or made
unauthorized trades.
Respondent reiterated this in its Statement of Answer.
5. No evidence of misrepresentation or fraudulent concealment

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02261
Award Page 5 of 8
The Statement of Claim alleges that Claimant “fraudulently concealed” risks
and repeatedly misrepresented the condition of the funds.
The Panel finds:
• No documentary evidence supporting these allegations.
• No testimony or records showing that Claimant made false statements.
• No evidence that Claimant advised the Customers to “hold” for improper
reasons.
• No evidence that Claimant concealed liquidity risks or market conditions.
Respondent confirmed that the allegations were false and that Claimant
acted appropriately.
6. Losses Were Caused by the 2013 Puerto Rico Bond Market Collapse
The Panel finds that:
• The Customers’ losses were caused by the unprecedented collapse of
the Puerto Rico bond market, not by any action or inaction of Claimant.
• The market event was outside the control of both Respondent and
Claimant.
• The Customers earned more than $538,000.00 in dividends and interest
and realized a net profit exceeding $320,000.00 during the relevant
period.
These facts contradict the Customers’ Statement of Claim assertion of
$200,000.00 in damages caused by Claimants.
7. The Customers continued to work with Claimant
Despite filing the arbitration, the Customers remained clients of Claimant,
which the Panel finds inconsistent with allegations of fraud, concealment, or
unsuitable recommendations.
8. Respondent fully supports expungement
Respondent stated in its Statement of Answer that:
• The allegations against Claimant were clearly erroneous and false.
• Claimant did not recommend concentration in Puerto Rico securities.
• Claimant recommended diversification.
• The accounts were non-discretionary.
• Claimant did not contribute to or participate in the settlement.
• Respondent filed the Form U4 disclosure solely due to regulatory
requirements.

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02261
Award Page 6 of 8
The Panel gives significant weight to Respondent’s unqualified support for
expungement.
Panel Conclusion:
Based on the testimony, documentary evidence, and Respondent’s
concessions, the Panel unanimously finds that:
• The allegations against Petitioner were clearly erroneous under Rule
13805(c)(9)(A)(i)a.
• The allegations were false under Rule 13805(c)(9)(A)(i)c.
• There is no evidence of unsuitable recommendations,
misrepresentation, or misconduct by Claimant.
• The losses were caused by external market conditions, not by Claimant.
Accordingly, the Panel recommends expungement of Occurrence Number
2270699 from Claimant’s CRD records pursuant to FINRA Rule
13805(c)(9)(A)(i)a and c.
2. Respondent is liable for and shall pay to Claimant the sum of $500.00 to reimburse Claimant
for the non-refundable portion of the filing fee previously paid to FINRA Dispute Resolution
Services.
FEES
Pursuant to the Code, the following fees are assessed:
Filing Fees
FINRA Dispute Resolution Services assessed a filing fee* for each claim:
Initial Claim Filing Fee =$ 2,000.00
*The filing fee is made up of a non-refundable and a refundable portion.
Member Fees
Member fees are assessed to each member firm that is a party in these proceedings or to the
member firm(s) that employed the associated person(s) at the time of the event(s) giving rise to
the dispute. Accordingly, as a party, Respondent is assessed the following:
Member Surcharge =$ 2,600.00
Member Process Fee =$ 5,005.00
Hearing Session Fees and Assessments
The Panel has assessed hearing session fees for each session conducted. A session is any
meeting between the parties and the Arbitrator(s), including a pre-hearing conference with the
Arbitrator(s), which lasts four (4) hours or less. Fees associated with these proceedings are:

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02261
Award Page 7 of 8
One (1) pre-hearing session with the Panel @ $1,725.00/session =$ 1,725.00
Pre-Hearing Conference: January 29, 2026 1 session
One (1) hearing session on expungement request @ $1,725.00/session =$ 1,725.00
Hearing: March 12, 2026 1 session
Total Hearing Session Fees =$ 3,450.00
The Panel has assessed the total hearing session fees to Respondent.
All balances are payable to FINRA Dispute Resolution Services and are due upon receipt.

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02261
Award Page 8 of 8
ARBITRATION PANEL
Mark T. Risner - Public Arbitrator, Presiding Chairperson
Roger C. Benson - Public Arbitrator
Sherry R. Wetsch - Public Arbitrator
I, the undersigned Arbitrator, do hereby affirm that I am the individual described herein and who
executed this instrument, which is my award.
Concurring Arbitrators' Signatures
